title: unordered_set
description: Same idea as set — unique values — but hash-based instead of sorted, trading order for typically much faster lookups.
objectives: Understand unordered_set as hash-based rather than sorted
Use the same insert()/find()/count()/erase() interface as set
Know why it's typically faster than set for lookups
Choose between set and unordered_set correctly

sections.text: 1. Recap: set Was Sorted, but O(log n)

set kept elements sorted automatically, using a balanced binary search tree, giving roughly logarithmic-time operations. unordered_set drops the sorting guarantee entirely, in exchange for typically much faster operations.
unordered_set stores unique values with no particular guaranteed order, using hashing internally to achieve average constant-time insertion, lookup, and removal.

insert(), count(), and duplicate handling all work identically to set — this example deliberately avoids printing every element in order, since unordered_set makes no promise about what order that would come out in.

Iterating an unordered_set with a range-based for loop is valid, but the order elements come out in is unspecified and can even change between runs. If sorted output matters, set is the right choice; if order genuinely doesn't matter and speed does, unordered_set is usually better.

Instead of comparing values against a sorted tree structure, unordered_set uses a hash function to convert each value into a number that determines roughly where it's stored — allowing insert(), find(), and count() to jump close to the right spot directly, rather than navigating down a tree. This is what gives average O(1) performance, versus set's O(log n).

rows: Choose when sorted iteration is needed
Choose when only fast membership checks matter

sections.text: For pure DSA problems that just need to answer "have I seen this value before?" as fast as possible, unordered_set is usually the better default — this is a very common pattern for detecting duplicates or checking membership efficiently.

sections.title: A guest list checked at the door, not read aloud alphabetically
sections.text: Recall set's alphabetical guest list. unordered_set is a guest list kept in whatever quick-lookup system gets a bouncer to "yes, they're on the list" or "no, they're not" almost instantly — nobody cares that the underlying storage isn't alphabetically organized, since nobody ever needs to read the whole list out loud in order.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Expecting unordered_set to iterate in insertion or sorted order — it guarantees neither.
Choosing unordered_set when sorted output is actually needed — set is the right tool for that.
Assuming unordered_set is always faster in every single case — average performance is O(1), but worst-case scenarios (rare, hash-related) can degrade this.
Forgetting #include <unordered_set> — it's a separate header from <set>.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What is the main difference between set and unordered_set?
questions.answer: set keeps elements automatically sorted with O(log n) operations via a balanced tree; unordered_set has no guaranteed order but typically achieves average O(1) operations via hashing.

questions.question: Why is unordered_set typically faster than set?
questions.answer: It uses a hash function to jump close to the right storage location directly, rather than navigating a sorted tree structure comparison by comparison.

questions.question: When would you choose set over unordered_set?
questions.answer: When elements need to be iterated in sorted order — unordered_set makes no ordering guarantee at all.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: unordered_set stores unique values just like set, with the same insert()/find()/count()/erase() interface, but drops the sorted-order guarantee in exchange for typically much faster operations — average O(1) instead of set's O(log n) — achieved through hashing rather than a balanced tree. Iterating it gives elements in an unspecified order that can even vary between runs, so set remains the right choice when sorted output matters. For pure membership-checking tasks common in DSA, like detecting duplicates quickly, unordered_set is usually the better default.

sections.title: Trick to Remember unordered_set
sections.text: unordered_set = the bouncer's quick-check list. Fast yes/no answer, no promise about order. set = the alphabetical guest list, slower, but organized.
